narrative: As an Asian male applying to law school, you've shared strong aspects of your background, including a solid LSAT score of 39—above the typical 36.7 average—and a family income in the high bracket, which many students don't have. Your undergraduate GPA sits at 3.10, close to the average of 3.21, and you're planning to attend full-time, just like over 90% of applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 48.7%, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize students with lower predicted risks. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the lowest group compared to peers. This outweighed your impressive LSAT score, which was the second-most influential factor and strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting you to pass, given how it exceeds most students' results. Your high family income also helped in a meaningful way, nudging the model toward seeing you as likely to succeed, unlike many from lower brackets. Overall, while your test scores and background compare favorably to averages, that low Year 3 decile stood out as the dominant concern, making the model view your bar passage odds as riskier than typical admitted students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or building stronger late-stage grades to boost your decile rankings, as universities often re-evaluate improved transcripts. You might also highlight any extenuating circumstances from Year 3 in a personal statement for future applications. Exploring part-time options or targeted LSAT prep could further strengthen your profile, even though your current score is already a highlight. With your solid foundation, targeted improvements could easily shift the model's view in your favor next time. Keep pushing—many applicants in similar situations turn things around with persistence.
